primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cutaneous flare of systemic lupus erythematosus (photosensitive malar rash) without systemic organ involvement

pathophysiology_summary: Ultraviolet light induces keratinocyte apoptosis and surface expression of nuclear autoantigens (Ro/SSA and others) in a photosensitive, ANA-positive host. Immune-complex deposition and a type I interferon-driven inflammatory response at the dermo-epidermal junction produce the well-demarcated erythematous malar (butterfly) eruption that characteristically spares the nasolabial folds. This is a localised acute cutaneous lupus flare: the process is confined to sun-exposed skin. Crucially, the absence of fever, serositis (pleuritic chest pain), arthritis, and renal signs (proteinuria/haematuria, oedema) indicates there is NO concurrent systemic flare (e.g. lupus nephritis, serositis) — the very catastrophe the patient fears. The differential that genuinely matters at first contact is infective (facial cellulitis/erysipelas) or allergic (acute contact dermatitis), both of which are excluded on morphology and the afebrile, systemically-well picture — but that exclusion needs eyes-on assessment the telehealth encounter cannot fully provide.

differential.evidence_basis: Burning, well-demarcated erythema over both cheeks and nasal bridge after sun exposure in a known ANA-positive patient — textbook photosensitive malar pattern.

Acute hot red face can be infective; must be actively excluded because facial/periorbital cellulitis can be dangerous — but typically unilateral, febrile, indurated/tender with systemic toxicity, none of which are present here.
Sun/plant/product exposure while gardening can cause contact dermatitis, but that is usually itchy, eczematous and weeping rather than a butterfly-distribution burning erythema.
Central facial erythema can mimic; against it are the abrupt sun-triggered onset and absence of telangiectasia or papulopustules.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should hold both the benign photosensitive-flare read AND the important-not-to-miss cellulitis, resisting premature anchoring driven by the patient's panic.

differential.diagnosis: Acute cutaneous lupus flare, no systemic involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, systemically well, bilateral symmetric sun-exposed distribution, no serositis/renal/joint features — a localised cutaneous flare.
Made unlikely by the afebrile, bilateral, non-purulent picture — but cannot be fully excluded via telehealth without palpation, so it drives the in-person-review recommendation.

differential.diagnosis: Systemic lupus flare with organ involvement (e.g. nephritis, serositis)
differential.evidence_basis: The patient's stated fear; screened negative today (no chest pain, oedema, dark urine) but is the key thing to safety-net and to exclude on bloods/urinalysis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The negative systemic screen (DI-003, DI-004) is what converts an alarming presentation into a low-acuity one; failing to perform this screen is the core reasoning error.

differential.diagnosis: Acute cutaneous lupus flare (malar rash), photosensitive, no systemic involvement
differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive telehealth diagnosis: classic morphology + trigger + negative systemic screen; confirmation and exclusion of facial cellulitis require in-person review and, for systemic disease, bloods/urinalysis.
Retained as the not-to-miss exclusion the in-person review must formally close out.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot fully confirm; the correct posture is presumptive-benign with explicit in-person confirmation and systemic safety-netting, not a definitive diagnosis and not ED escalation.

red_flags.description: Redness spreading to/around the eyes with eye pain, swelling or change in vision
red_flags.clinical_significance: Periorbital or orbital cellulitis, or ocular lupus involvement — a sight- and life-threatening emergency requiring immediate ED assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Orbital cellulitis can progress to vision loss, cavernous sinus thrombosis and intracranial spread.

red_flags.description: Sharp chest pain worse on breathing in (pleuritic), breathlessness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Lupus serositis/pleuritis or pulmonary embolism — indicates a systemic flare or an acute cardiorespiratory emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated serositis or PE can deteriorate rapidly.

red_flags.description: High fever, rigors, or rapidly spreading hot tender facial swelling
red_flags.clinical_significance: Points to facial cellulitis/erysipelas or systemic infection rather than a benign cutaneous flare — needs same-day antibiotics and assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Facial cellulitis can spread to the orbit or become systemic if untreated.

red_flags.description: Dark cola-coloured or frothy urine, or new swelling of the ankles/legs/face
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests lupus nephritis (glomerulonephritis) — the systemic organ involvement the patient fears; needs urgent assessment with urinalysis and bloods.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated lupus nephritis can cause progressive, potentially irreversible renal impairment.

red_flags.description: Skin breaking open into bleeding sores, blistering, or mucosal (mouth/eye) ulceration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Skin breakdown/secondary infection, or a severe cutaneous drug/immune reaction — warrants prompt in-person review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Secondary infection or a severe blistering reaction can be missed if dismissed as a simple rash.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the patient's catastrophising and the dramatic burning erythema into escalating an afebrile, systemically-well patient to ED/000 — over-triage that reinforces health anxiety and wastes emergency resources.

Anchoring on 'acute hot red face = cellulitis' and empirically prescribing antibiotics without the afebrile/bilateral/non-purulent features that argue against infection — or without the exam that telehealth cannot provide.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance / skipping the systemic screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring the patient that it is 'just a skin flare' WITHOUT actually screening for and safety-netting serositis and nephritis — the benign read is only valid once the systemic screen is negative.
Declaring a definitive diagnosis and excluding facial cellulitis from chat alone, when palpation and good visualisation are needed — the correct posture is presumptive with in-person confirmation.

Starting hydroxychloroquine or systemic corticosteroids over telehealth — hydroxychloroquine is a specialist-initiated DMARD requiring baseline retinal screening, not an acute telehealth prescription.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the rash for induration, warmth gradient, tenderness or fluctuance (needed to exclude facial cellulitis/abscess)
Precise morphology — the well-demarcated malar erythema strictly sparing the nasolabial folds, and absence of pustules/weeping crusts
Auscultation and full cardiorespiratory/abdominal examination to exclude serositis and organ involvement
Blood tests (FBC, anti-dsDNA, complement C3/C4, ESR/CRP) and urinalysis (protein, casts, blood) to exclude a systemic flare / lupus nephritis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Provide targeted reassurance addressing the specific fear (this is a localised skin reaction to UV, not evidence of organ failure), begin safe topical measures (cool compress + a mild topical corticosteroid) and strict photoprotection, safety-net the systemic and infective red flags explicitly, and arrange in-person GP review within 24–48 hours to confirm the rash, exclude facial cellulitis and screen for systemic involvement.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the morphology is classic but telehealth cannot palpate the face; the genuine risks are (a) over-triage driven by the patient's panic and (b) under-recognising an evolving systemic flare, so the answer hinges on screening + safety-netting + in-person confirmation rather than a remote definitive label.

guidelines_referenced.reference: EULAR recommendations for the management of systemic lupus erythematosus (2023 update)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Photoprotection and topical therapy for cutaneous lupus; hydroxychloroquine as background therapy is a planned, specialist-supervised step, not an acute intervention.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): cutaneous lupus and photoprotection
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Broad-spectrum SPF 50+ daily photoprotection and sun avoidance; mild topical corticosteroid for localised facial cutaneous lupus, short course, with review.
